speaker: interviewer
text: This is June 6, 1996 interview with Sonja Berkelhammer nee Rosen. Interviewer is Lisa Newman in Toronto, Ontario, Canada. The interview is in English. June 6, 1996 interview with Sonja Berkelhammer nee Rosen. Interviewer is Lisa Newman in Toronto, Ontario, Canada. Interview is in English. And you are?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Sonja Berkelhammer nee Rosen. I was born April 25, 1927 in Koszyce, Poland before of war.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chaim Raiselman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ita Raiselman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as well as Raiselman, they were known as Rosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You told me that your father, when you were very small, went to Canada.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you know about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was very small. I can't remember how the looked or what he was like. And he died here in Canada on sickness pneumonia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was he in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Canada, Winnipeg.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did he die?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was maybe 31 or 32 years old when he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you yourself would have been about three?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know anything more about the circumstances of his death?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what work did he do here in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a book keeper. And he also had a store.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know, had he planned to bring your mother and yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what then happened when your mother was widowed and she had you as a small child?

speaker: interviewee
text: She decided to stay in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she moved?

speaker: interviewee
text: No She moved from Koszyce to Tuczyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was in Tuczyn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had a big, beautiful house and a store. And she operated a store.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's parents were living there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Aunts, uncles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, aunts, uncles, yes. One uncle was in Canada. One uncle was in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your mother's parents' names, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Raiselman, too, Rosen.

speaker: interviewer
text: You told me that your mother and father were actually cousins.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And besides yourself in the family, were there other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Later, my mother remarried. And I had a sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name of the man she married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sam Kramer.

speaker: interviewer
text: They married in Tuczyn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it wasn't sort of a wedding. It was just by a rabbi, or acknowledgement.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as a little girl, you grew up in this home with your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Tuczyn. Did anybody else live there with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your grandparents lived in another house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, just beside us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you visit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I visit very often. Actually, my real grandmother died years ago. That was the second wife of Mr. Reuben Raiselman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sara. She was killed by the Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like to visit your grandparents' house when you were little?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very happy occasion.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my grandfather used to give me money, used to talk to me. He used to play with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was music in their home, in your grandparents' home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Radio.

speaker: interviewer
text: Radio. What did you listen to on the radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, songs, music. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] No television. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a garden?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, was a garden. My grandfather had a garden. We had two gardens from both sides of the house. It was a very beautiful home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the garden. What was in it?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the gardens, there were all kinds of fruit and vegetables.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember which fruits?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, cherries, apples, plums.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you help in the garden at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was too small.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did the work in the garden?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and some help she had.

speaker: interviewer
text: She had help in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: She hired help for the gardens.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she have help in the house as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She-- she hired people to come and clean the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she hire anybody to look after you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you spend time with her in the store then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I helped her in the store, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember doing in the store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, selling things to people, talking to people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you sell for instance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chocolates, mostly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get to have some?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were the chocolates made, did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Krakow, Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were some from outside Poland, too, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just from Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And people came to the store from where, from just the town of Tuczyn or farther away?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the villages nearby.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they come, on what?

speaker: interviewee
text: By horses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Horse and buggy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There was no other transportation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember riding in the horse and buggy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very enjoyable.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the winter, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the winter, in the summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was winter like? What-- what are your memories of winter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the winters were cold, just like in Winnipeg.

speaker: interviewer
text: A lot of snow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lot of snow, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did children do to play and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we had all kinds of toys, like-- I don't know, Polish you say [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know how you say it in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does it do?

speaker: interviewee
text: You sit down and--

speaker: interviewer
text: And slide on the snow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, a sleigh?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think we call it a sleigh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Someone pulls you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who pulled you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The kids. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your friends mostly Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, German. We lived in a suburb. And there were German neighbors.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the suburb?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So you learned German as a little girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: As well as Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: As well as Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish I learned in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: In school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about going to school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, school was very interesting. I was a very good pupil. I was of the best. I had the best marks in the class. I was very high leveled. And the teachers were very happy to have me. And it was very interesting school for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you like to learn about especially? What were your favorites?

speaker: interviewee
text: Literature.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that international? What literature did you study?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish literature.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I think you told me you studied music.

speaker: interviewee
text: I took piano. But I'm not that educated in music, just a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you like to write songs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you, as a young person, write songs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And play them or sing them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sing them, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever perform?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where would you sing these songs that you made up?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of sports did you like to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE], gymnastics.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go on hikes or trips with your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in particular, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think you told me you were a member of Betar.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of things did you do with that group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we used to march and all kinds of-- and we were talk about Jabotinsky. That he made the group.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever think that you might live in Israel, in Palestine then?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anybody who went to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, mostly got killed. Because we had mass-- mass murder. We didn't have concentration camps. Everybody got killed. There were-- people were taken to the ghettos and they kill them all.

speaker: interviewer
text: But before the war, before Hitler, did you know people who went to Palestine early on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very few, very few.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war, before things began to change, what were your hopes and dreams for your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I was hoping to come to Canada. Because I had an uncle here, uncle and children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you imagine your life would be like in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, wonderful.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what would be wonderful?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, a new life, starting a new life. Everybody was thinking about Canada, America, something new.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you receive letters from your uncle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He always used to write letters.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what did he write to you about Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that he has a store. And he's doing very well. And the children are very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: When things began to change, what did you notice in Tuczyn? What was different?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean when start to change?

speaker: interviewer
text: When Hitler began to have power.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a lot of people didn't believe that he killed Jewish people. Till the last minute he was taking them to the ghetto to kill them, they didn't believe that they're going to die.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you yourself think?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I knew better that they're going to kill them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the German kids that live near me told me that Hitler kills Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I was-- I thought it's pretty awful, pretty horrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your friends offer to hide you or help you escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a woman that wanted to take me to Germany under German name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened with that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't want to go. I didn't want to leave my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your mother think about all this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she didn't want to give me, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she believe that Hitler would kill Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at the beginning, she didn't want to believe. But later, she believed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember telling her what your German friends told you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her reaction?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pretty awful.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about Canada at that time? Were you thinking to leave at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when-- when Hitler came to power, there was no way to go out or in. I mean, you were just closed in. You were just where you were. There was no place to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were how old at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Around 10, I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then how did things change in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] I mean, when Hitler came to power, he had a factory were making thread, linen, thread. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: That was your mother's business?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. When Hitler came to power, we were working in a factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or, he-- the Nazis placed you in a factory to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And were making linen, thread for the Nazi army. Some made socks and sweaters. And then one day, they said, there was-- the factory will be closed. They're going to take us to the ghetto. So I knew that's a time to escape.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were working in that factory, you were still living at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember the name of the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: The factory had no name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a factory that existed before--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the Nazis?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: They started it?

speaker: interviewee
text: They started it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So instead of going to the ghetto, what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I decided to run away. I was standing in front of the house and watching are they coming to take us. And then when I saw approaching the Nazi officers, we started to run. So they started to shoot after us. And we were running. They were running after us. But then they returned, and they let us run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: 'We' is you and who?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me, my mother, my aunt, my uncle, and neighbors.

speaker: interviewer
text: It sounds like you were kind of a leader in this little group.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You were young to be making those decisions.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me how it went from there.

speaker: interviewee
text: We run away. And the first night, we stayed at-- at Polish-- at Polish people in a village on a hideout. And then they started to burn villages where they were finding Jewish people. So I decided we go to the woods. instead of staying with the Polish people. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: The woods was [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was that from Tuczyn?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was quite a miles away.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Well, I mean, you just go to the woods that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: You walked?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take to walk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: A whole day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a whole day, hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did-- what did you do for food?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were begging food from the farmers.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they react?

speaker: interviewee
text: They give us food. Sometimes they give us work, like making sweaters and socks.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were people who knew you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were some of them Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The Germans were in Germany. Because when Hitler made a pact with Russia, all the German people went to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that included the families of the people, the children you were friendly with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other families as well that went to the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was lots of Jewish people in the woods. But the Nazis used to come and look for Jewish people and kill them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me some of those memories. What do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember one time we were at-- at the hide out. Because the hide out was like you dug out a hole. And then on top, you put ground on. And the Nazis were standing on our ground. So there was a little girl, my aunt's little girl started to cry. So she started to choke her and wanted to kill her, because she was afraid the Nazis will find out our hide out. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your cousin's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sonja.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was this Sonja?.

speaker: interviewee
text: One-year-old, two years old, something like that. So I didn't like my aunt to choke her to death. And I whispered, [NON-ENGLISH]. So that Nazis said [NON-ENGLISH] And they started to laugh. And they let the ground.

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH] meaning?

speaker: interviewee
text: A cat.

speaker: interviewer
text: A cat. [LAUGHS] And they just left?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they just left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your cousin survive the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She is now in Israel. Her kids are in the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what else you remember from--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we were collecting berries. Sometimes saw wolves. Sometimes they looked horrible. Sometimes they were calm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they attack?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. And we were-- every day, we used to go out for bread to the farmers, the bag bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was that? How long did it take to walk?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were farmers who knew that there were Jews in the woods.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were they ever-- did they ever betray the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we used to know all those farmers. But a lot of times, these people were betraying some Jewish hide outs, where the Jewish people were hiding. So the Nazis used to come and kill them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you explain that you didn't get betrayed?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, just a coincidence.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember other close calls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember other close calls where you were almost found?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were begging bread, the farmers knew that you were Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't pretend to be--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --not Jews. How did you keep track of time and dates during that period? How did you know what day it was? Did you ever see a newspaper?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [LAUGHS] Nobody saw a newspaper.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what was going on in the war, how things were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] We were liberated in 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in the words for how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years, 1942 to 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you sleep underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the ground or on--

speaker: interviewee
text: On the ground. We used to wash our clothes there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get water to wash them?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the river.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were near a river?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which river?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about in winter? How did you keep warm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we just were cold I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have fire?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We made fire.

speaker: interviewer
text: For cooking, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were glad that we had enough bread, never mind cooking. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever catch animals to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember being hungry?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't hungry, no. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The farmers gave us enough. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My mother used to go with her sister. And there was men. They used to go begging for bread. They always gave them enough food.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else do you remember from being in the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: What else? Well, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] we were collecting berries in the spring time-- the summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your grandparents were not with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My grandfather committed suicide.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Nazis came, he committed suicide.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: With a pill.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reaction in the family to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were very, of course, very disappointed and very-- how should I say-- mourning-- in mourning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you sit Shiva for him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your grandmother, what did she do?

speaker: interviewee
text: She stayed, went to the ghetto. And the ghetto means she got killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where or when she was killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We don't know where they took them. But all those people that run away from the place when they were taking them said they got killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the last time you saw her?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what she said?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in particular, but she knew it's very bad. I mean, people started to know that Hitler is killing Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she try to come to the forest, to the woods, with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in the woods, did you think that you would survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I hoped to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you continue to write songs?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes? Do you remember any of them that you wrote at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not in particular, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you used to write them down?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did those survive any of those notes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those songs didn't survive. But then we came back from the woods, my diary was left in my house. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I found my diary.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you actually still have your childhood diary?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't have it. But I had it then.

speaker: interviewer
text: And had someone been living in your house while you were in the woods.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was-- it remained empty?

speaker: interviewee
text: Empty. Everything was taken out of the house. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took those things?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever-- were you ever able to get your property back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, before we run away, we give a lot our goods to the-- to the farmers. And later after the war, they gave us all back, all kinds of linen and clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to take a short break.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you need to walk around?
